title: Attention as a moral resource
summary: Argue that attention is closer to a moral resource than to a currency, and ask whose attention deserves yours.
prompt: Write a 280–360-word argumentative essay reframing 'attention' from currency to moral resource. Take a clear position. Use hedging ('What I want to suggest, tentatively…'), at least one cleft, and a paragraph that distinguishes three features of the thing you are defining. Avoid AI-style filler.
hintStarters: There is something faintly humiliating about…
What I want to suggest, tentatively, is…
Once attention is seen this way,…
Some people, some platforms, some causes have done very well from…

hintVocab.meaningVi: khung tự cải thiện bản thân

sampleText:
There is something faintly humiliating about the language we have inherited for talking about attention. We speak of paying it, spending it, capturing it — as if it were money in a wallet, or, more recently, as if it were a small animal that platforms compete to trap. The metaphor is not idle. It shapes what we feel obliged to do with our minds. If attention is currency, then giving it away is generosity; withholding it begins to feel mean. The problem is that the metaphor is wrong.

What I want to suggest, tentatively, is that attention is more usefully understood as a moral resource — closer in nature to time spent with the sick or words spoken in private about a friend than to anything purchasable. Three features distinguish it. It cannot be recovered: an hour given to a stranger's outrage is not refundable from the hour you might have given to a parent. It is not divisible without loss: the half-attention we offer during a phone scroll is not half a gift; in many cases it is worse than no gift at all. And, crucially, it is consequential for the dignity of the one who receives it. To be attended to with care is to be told, without words, that one is real.

Once attention is seen this way, the ethical question shifts. The right question is not "how do I focus more?" — a self-improvement frame that conveniently keeps the agenda inside the individual skull. The better question, harder to ask, is whose attention deserves yours. Some people, some platforms, some causes have done very well from never being asked that question. They will not enjoy it being asked now. That is, perhaps, the clearest sign that it is the right one.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: phép ẩn dụ đó không vô hại
khung tự cải thiện bản thân

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Hedging at C1
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What I want to suggest, tentatively, is…' và 'is, perhaps, the clearest sign' dùng các từ như 'tentatively', 'perhaps' để làm dịu nhận định mà không yếu đi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What I want to suggest, tentatively, is…' and 'is, perhaps, the clearest sign' use 'tentatively' and 'perhaps' to soften the claim without weakening it.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Three-feature definition pattern
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Three features distinguish it. It cannot be recovered… It is not divisible without loss… And, crucially, it is consequential…' là cấu trúc chuẩn để định nghĩa một khái niệm trong văn nghị luận.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Three features distinguish it. It cannot be recovered… It is not divisible without loss… And, crucially, it is consequential…' is a standard pattern for defining a concept in argumentative writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có điều gì đó hơi đáng xấu hổ trong cách ngôn ngữ ta thừa hưởng để nói về sự chú ý. Ta nói trả nó, tiêu nó, bắt giữ nó — như thể nó là tiền trong ví, hoặc, gần đây hơn, như thể nó là một con vật nhỏ mà các nền tảng đua nhau bẫy. Phép ẩn dụ ấy không vô hại. Nó định hình điều ta cảm thấy có nghĩa vụ làm với tâm trí mình. Nếu sự chú ý là tiền, thì cho nó đi là hào phóng; giữ lại bắt đầu thấy có vẻ keo kiệt. Vấn đề là phép ẩn dụ đó sai.

Điều tôi muốn đề xuất, một cách dè dặt, là sự chú ý nên được hiểu rõ hơn như một nguồn lực đạo đức — gần với thời gian ngồi cạnh người bệnh hay những lời nói riêng tư về một người bạn, hơn là với bất cứ thứ gì có thể mua được. Có ba đặc điểm phân biệt nó. Nó không thể lấy lại: một giờ dành cho cơn giận của người lạ không hoàn trả được từ một giờ ta có thể dành cho cha mẹ. Nó không thể chia mà không mất giá: nửa-chú-ý mà ta đưa ra khi lướt điện thoại không phải là nửa món quà; trong nhiều trường hợp nó còn tệ hơn không có quà nào. Và, quan trọng nhất, nó có hệ quả đối với phẩm giá của người nhận. Được chú ý với sự ân cần là được nói, không bằng lời, rằng mình là thật.

Khi sự chú ý được nhìn theo cách đó, câu hỏi đạo đức chuyển hướng. Câu hỏi đúng không phải là "làm sao để tập trung hơn?" — một khung tự cải thiện bản thân tiện lợi giữ toàn bộ vấn đề bên trong cái hộp sọ cá nhân. Câu hỏi tốt hơn, khó hỏi hơn, là: sự chú ý của bạn xứng đáng với ai? Một số người, một số nền tảng, một số mục tiêu đã sống rất khoẻ vì chưa bao giờ bị hỏi câu ấy. Họ sẽ không thích nó được hỏi bây giờ. Đó, có lẽ, là dấu hiệu rõ nhất rằng nó là câu hỏi đúng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges most appropriately at C1 level without sounding weak?
options: I am not sure but maybe possibly attention could be a resource of some kind.
What I want to suggest, tentatively, is that attention is more usefully understood as a moral resource.
Attention is, definitely, one hundred percent a moral resource and nothing else.
Attention may or may not be a kind of resource or not.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'tentatively' inside the cleft signals careful claim-making without retreating from the position.
mcQuestions.hint: C1 hedging adds one careful adverb, not a pile of qualifiers.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a cleft sentence to focus attention on the key idea?
options: It is whose attention deserves yours that is the better question, harder to ask.
Whose attention deserves yours, it is the better question.
The better question, the harder to ask, whose attention deserves yours.
Better question that whose deserves yours your attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X that…' is a classic cleft for focusing on X (the key question).
mcQuestions.hint: A cleft pushes the key noun phrase to the front using 'It is … that …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that draws a sharp distinction between two framings, in C1 register.
options: The right question is not 'how do I focus more?' — a self-improvement frame that conveniently keeps the agenda inside the individual skull. The better question, harder to ask, is whose attention deserves yours.
Focus more is not the right question. Whose attention is also a question.
Right question I focus more no, who attention deserves your is better.
Focusing improvement self is best for attention questions today.
mcQuestions.explanation: A juxtaposes the wrong question and the right question with parallel structure and an em-dashed gloss.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a sentence that uses parallel structure and an em-dashed clarification.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel three-part listing most effectively?
options: Some people, some platforms, some causes have done very well from never being asked that question.
Some people and platforms and also causes did well never asked that.
People some, platforms some, causes some — well doing without question asking.
Many people platforms causes asking never that question well.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Some X, some Y, some Z' is the parallel listing pattern; it gives the sentence rhetorical weight.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for three noun phrases starting with the same word.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which closing sentence avoids AI-style filler and lands the argument?
options: In conclusion, attention is one of the most important resources of modern life.
Throughout history, humans have always struggled with where to look.
That is, perhaps, the clearest sign that it is the right one.
It is important to note that focusing well is a personal skill.
mcQuestions.explanation: C completes the rhetorical setup of the paragraph — a hedged, controlled landing. The others are banned filler.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'in conclusion', 'throughout history', 'it is important to note'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Name one person or platform that has had a great deal of your attention this week without earning it. What would change in your life if you stopped giving it?
